en.extract:
The cinnamon-breasted bunting (Emberiza tahapisi) or cinnamon-breasted rock-bunting is a species of bird in the family Emberizidae. It is widespread in Africa south of the Sahara.


Taxonomy and systematics
The cinnamon-breasted bunting was formally described in 1836 by the Scottish zoologist Andrew Smith under the current binomial name Emberiza tahapisi. His description was based on specimens found near the source of the Vaal River in South Africa. The specific epithet tahapisi is from the Tswana word "Thagapitse" that is used for various finches in the family Emberizidae. 
The species is also known as the cinnamon-breasted rock bunting or African rock bunting. They are small passerines  in the genus Emberiza, which consists of other Old World buntings. There have been some discrepancies regarding the relation to other species and subspecies. Six subspecies of the cinnamon-breasted bunting have been recognized, including E. t. goslingi, E. t. septemstriata, E. t. arabica, E. t. insularis, E. t. tahapisi, and E. t. nivenorum. However, recent mitochondrial analyses of African buntings suggest that the Gosling's bunting (Emberiza goslingi) should be treated as a separate species. E. t. septemstriata is also believed to be a hybrid between the nominate species and Gosling's bunting. Alternate scientific naming includes Fringillaria tahapisi.


Description
They weigh between 11.6 and 21 grams with a length of 13–15 centimeters. Although plumage differs between juveniles, males, and females, cinnamon-breasted buntings can be identified by their striped head and cinnamon brown underparts. Males have black and white stripes whereas the female stripes are less dark and browner in color. The throat patch is uniformly black on males but smaller, grayish, and densely blotched on females. Juveniles are similar to females but with buffier head stripes and a paler, browner throat. After the breeding season, juveniles moult partially and adults moult completely so all primaries and secondaries are the same age.
Cinnamon-breasted bunting songs are composed of short, high-pitched, rapid trills. Males are known to have eight distinct songs with 40 discrete syllables. Song similarity decreases with distance between local populations but all end with a characteristic final whistle syllable. Individuals perform songs from elevated positions such as from trees, bushes, or rocks.


Distribution and habitat
Having a very widespread distribution, cinnamon-breasted buntings are found across most of mainland sub-Saharan Africa but avoiding deserts, equatorial forest, and the high altitudes of mountains. They can be found in rocky, lightly wooded hillsides with sparse vegetation and bare soil but can sometimes be found in woodlands. There is some degree of partial migration with certain populations moving north during the rainy season and south during the dry season as well as moving to lower elevations during winter.


Behavior and ecology
Breeding time varies depending on the rainy season of the region. In arid regions with light rainfall, the breeding season will occur throughout the rainy season but in regions with heavier rainfall, the breeding season occurs at the end of the rainy season. They are monogamous but solitary nesters. The nest is built from twigs and grass and typically placed on the ground in a shaded area. Incubation and care for young is performed by both males and females. Incubation lasts 12–14 days and offspring fledge 14–16 days post-hatch but continue to feed in parental territory for three weeks. The clutch size is 2–4 and the eggs are greenish- or bluish-white with dark brown or reddish-brown speckles.

Cinnamon-breasted buntings are ground gleaners that primarily eat seeds but have also been known to eat insects, especially in captivity. They will also sometimes jump to pull seeds. Chicks are fed seeds and insects by parents.
They are commonly spotted alone or in pairs but also flock in groups of 3–4, and even 6–8 but less frequently so. However, migrating birds can be found in flocks of 40. Tail flicks are a common behavior that defines Emberiza and is used while hopping on the ground to make them more conspicuous to mates and other birds. Courtship behavior includes males chasing the female through the air, males bringing nest materials to nest and making "nest shaping movements", and females quivering their wings while making a special repeating eep sound. Aggressive behavior includes a head forward position with bill snapping or gaping, with bill snapping occurring more frequently when fear is stronger than aggression. When fighting, special chaa, eee, and chu chatter sounds are made. Fear responses include freezing and observing when the perceived threat level is low, fleeing, and making alarm calls. Higher intensity behavior includes mobbing.


Relationship to humans
Cinnamon-breasted buntings may occasionally enter towns and in some countries they are caught for cagebird trade but adapt well in captivity.


Status
According to the IUCN, they are categorized as least concern. However, their range has expanded since 1985 due to climate change and habitat fragmentation.


References

The '''cinnamon-breasted bunting''' ('''''Emberiza tahapisi''''') or '''cinnamon-breasted rock-bunting''' is a species of bird in the family Emberizidae. It is widespread in Africa south of the Sahara.

==Taxonomy and systematics==
The cinnamon-breasted bunting was [[species description|formally described]] in 1836 by the Scottish zoologist [[Andrew Smith (zoologist)|Andrew Smith]] under the current [[binomial name]] ''Emberiza tahapisi''. His description was based on specimens found near the source of the [[Vaal River]] in South Africa.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Smith | first=Andrew | author-link=Andrew Smith (zoologist) | date=1836 | title=Report of the Expedition for Exploring Central Africa from the Cape of Good Hope | location=Cape Town | publisher=Printed at the Government Gazette Office | page=48 | url=https://archive.org/details/reportexpeditio00afrigoog/page/n54/mode/1up }}</ref> The specific epithet ''tahapisi'' is from the [[Tswana language|Tswana]] word "Thagapitse" that is used for various finches in the family [[Emberizidae]].<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=378 |url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n378/mode/1up }}</ref> 

The species is also known as the cinnamon-breasted rock bunting or African rock bunting.<ref name=":0">{{ cite book | last= | first= | year=2011 | chapter=Cinnamon-breasted Bunting | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | volume=16: Tanagers to New World Blackbirds | location=Barcelona, Spain | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=978-84-96553-78-1 | page=552 | chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0016unse/page/552/mode/1up | chapter-url-access=registration }}</ref> They are small [[passerine]]s  in the genus ''[[Emberiza]]'', which consists of other [[Old World]] [[Emberiza|buntings]].<ref name=":1">{{ cite journal | last1=Alström | first1=P. | last2=Olsson | first2=U. | last3=Lei | first3=F. | last4=Wang | first4=H. | last5=Gao | first5=W. | last6=Sundberg | first6=P. | year=2008 | title=Phylogeny and classification of the Old World Emberizini (Aves, Passeriformes) | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=47 | issue=3 | pages=960–973 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2007.12.007 | pmid=18411062 | bibcode=2008MolPE..47..960A }}</ref><ref name=":2">Byers, C., U. Olsson and J. Curson. 1995. ''Sparrows and buntings''. Boston, MA: Houghton Mifflin Company.</ref> There have been some discrepancies regarding the relation to other species and [[subspecies]]. Six subspecies of the cinnamon-breasted bunting have been recognized, including ''E. t. goslingi, E. t. septemstriata, E. t. arabica, E. t. insularis, E. t. tahapisi,'' and ''E. t. nivenorum''.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> However, recent mitochondrial analyses of African buntings suggest that the Gosling's bunting (''Emberiza goslingi'') should be treated as a separate species.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal | last1=Olsson | first1=U. | last2=Yosef | first2=R. | last3=Alström | first3=P. | date=2013 | title=Assessment of species limits in African 'brown buntings' (''Emberiza'', Passeriformes) based on mitochondrial and nuclear sequence data | journal=Ibis | volume=155 | issue=3 | pages=534–543 | doi=10.1111/ibi.12044}}</ref> ''E. t. septemstriata'' is also believed to be a hybrid between the nominate species and Gosling's bunting.<ref name=":0" /> Alternate scientific naming includes ''Fringillaria tahapisi''.<ref name=":2" />

==Description==
They weigh between 11.6 and 21 grams with a length of 13–15 centimeters.<ref name=":0" /> Although plumage differs between juveniles, males, and females,<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":4">{{ cite book | last1=Fry | first1=C.H. | last2=Keith | first2=S. | last3=Woodcock | first3=M. | last4=Willis | first4=I. | year=2004 | title=The Birds of Africa | volume=VII: Sparrows to Buntings | location=London | publisher=Christopher Helm | isbn=978-0-7136-6531-4 | pages=574–578 }}</ref> cinnamon-breasted buntings can be identified by their striped head and cinnamon brown underparts.<ref name=":4" /> Males have black and white stripes whereas the female stripes are less dark and browner in color.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":4" /> The throat patch is uniformly black on males but smaller, grayish, and densely blotched on females.<ref name=":4" /> Juveniles are similar to females but with buffier head stripes and a paler, browner throat.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":4" /> After the breeding season, juveniles [[Moulting|moult]] partially and adults moult completely so all primaries and secondaries are the same age.<ref name=":4" />

Cinnamon-breasted bunting songs are composed of short, high-pitched, rapid trills.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":4" /> Males are known to have eight distinct songs with 40 discrete syllables.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal | last=Osiejuk | first=Tomasz S. | date=2011 | title=The song of the Cinnamon-breasted Bunting, ''Emberiza tahapisi'', in the Bamenda Highlands (NW Cameroon) | journal=Journal of Ornithology | volume=152 | issue=3 | pages=651–659 | doi=10.1007/s10336-010-0626-5| doi-access=free | url=https://hal.science/hal-00659906/file/PEER_stage2_10.1007%252Fs10336-010-0626-5.pdf }}</ref> Song similarity decreases with distance between local populations but all end with a characteristic final whistle syllable.<ref name=":5" /> Individuals perform songs from elevated positions such as from trees, bushes, or rocks.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":5" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
Having a very widespread distribution, cinnamon-breasted buntings are found across most of mainland [[sub-Saharan Africa]]<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":4" /><ref name=":6">{{ cite book | last=Dean | first=W.R.J. | chapter=Rock Bunting | editor1-last=Harrison | editor1-first=J.A. | editor2-last=Allan | editor2-first=D.G. | editor3-last=Underhill | editor3-first=L.G. | editor4-last=Herremans | editor4-first=M. | editor5-last=Tree | editor5-first=A.J. | editor6-last=Parker | editor6-first=V. | editor7-last=Brown | editor7-first=C.J. | year=1997 | title=The Atlas of Southern African Birds  | volume=2: Passerines | location=Johannesburg  | publisher=BirdLife South Africa | page=680 | isbn=978-0-620-20731-7 | chapter-url=https://sabap2.birdmap.africa/docs/sabap1/886.pdf }}</ref> but avoiding deserts, equatorial forest, and the high altitudes of mountains.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":4" /> They can be found in rocky, lightly wooded hillsides with sparse vegetation and bare soil but can sometimes be found in woodlands.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":2" /><ref name=":4" /><ref name=":6" /> There is some degree of partial migration with certain populations moving north during the rainy season and south during the dry season as well as moving to lower elevations during winter.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":6" />

== Behavior and ecology ==
Breeding time varies depending on the rainy season of the region.<ref name=":4" /> In arid regions with light rainfall, the breeding season will occur throughout the rainy season but in regions with heavier rainfall, the breeding season occurs at the end of the rainy season.<ref name=":4" /> They are monogamous but solitary nesters.<ref name=":0" /> The nest is built from twigs and grass and typically placed on the ground in a shaded area.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":4" /> [[Egg incubation|Incubation]] and care for young is performed by both males and females.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":4" /> Incubation lasts 12–14 days and offspring [[fledge]] 14–16 days post-hatch<ref name=":4" /> but continue to feed in parental territory for three weeks.<ref name=":0" /> The clutch size is 2–4 and the eggs are greenish- or bluish-white with dark brown or reddish-brown speckles.<ref name=":4" />

Cinnamon-breasted buntings are ground gleaners that primarily eat seeds but have also been known to eat insects, especially in captivity.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":3" /> They will also sometimes jump to pull seeds.<ref name=":0" /> Chicks are fed seeds and insects by parents.<ref name=":0" />[[File:Cinnamon-breasted Bunting (Emberiza tahapisi) (16013897374).jpg|thumb|255x255px|Feeding on grass seed in the [[Kruger National Park|Kruger N. P.]], South Africa|alt=|left]]

They are commonly spotted alone or in pairs but also flock in groups of 3–4, and even 6–8 but less frequently so.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":6" /> However, migrating birds can be found in flocks of 40.<ref name=":0" /> Tail flicks are a common behavior that defines ''Emberiza'' and is used while hopping on the ground to make them more conspicuous to mates and other birds.<ref>{{Cite journal | last=Andrew | first=R. J. | date=1956 | title=Intention movements of flight in certain passerines, and their use in systematics | journal=Behaviour | jstor=4532855 | volume=10 | issue=1/2 | pages=179–204| doi=10.1163/156853956X00147 }}</ref> [[Courtship display|Courtship behavior]] includes males chasing the female through the air,<ref name=":0" /> males bringing nest materials to nest and making "nest shaping movements",<ref name=":7">{{Cite journal | last=Andrew | first=R. J. | date=1956 | title=The aggressive and courtship behaviour of certain Emberizines | journal=Behaviour | jstor=4532860 | volume=10 | issue=3/4 | pages=255–308| doi=10.1163/156853956X00183 }}</ref> and females quivering their wings while making a special repeating ''eep'' sound.<ref name=":7" /> Aggressive behavior includes a head forward position with bill snapping or gaping, with bill snapping occurring more frequently when fear is stronger than aggression.<ref name=":7" /> When fighting, special ''chaa'', ''eee'', and ''chu'' chatter sounds are made.<ref name=":7" /> Fear responses include freezing and observing when the perceived threat level is low, fleeing, and making alarm calls. Higher intensity behavior includes [[Mobbing (animal behavior)|mobbing]].<ref>{{Cite journal | last=Andrew | first=R. J. | date=1956 | title=Fear responses in ''Emberiza'' spp. | journal=British Journal of Animal Behaviour | volume=4 | issue=4 | pages=125–132 | doi=10.1016/S0950-5601(56)80106-8}}</ref>

== Relationship to humans ==
Cinnamon-breasted buntings may occasionally enter towns<ref name=":4" /> and in some countries they are caught for [[Cage-bird trade|cagebird trade]] but adapt well in captivity.<ref name=":0" />

== Status ==
According to the IUCN, they are categorized as least concern.<ref name="iucn status 18 November 2021" /> However, their range has expanded since 1985 due to [[Climate change (general concept)|climate change]] and [[habitat fragmentation]].<ref name=":0" />
